metadata.description: Golden cases for neonatal growth and severity scoring
metadata.source: Fenton TR 2013 growth curves, SNAP-II scoring system, AAP clinical data

neonatal_calculations.clinical_scenario: 32-week gestational age preterm infant at birth, normal growth
neonatal_calculations.source: Fenton TR. A new growth chart for preterm infants. Arch Dis Child Fetal Neonatal Ed. 2013;98(5):F394-F398
neonatal_calculations.calculation_method: Weight at 32 weeks: ~1.5 kg at 50th percentile for male preterm infant
neonatal_calculations.notes: Fenton charts are standard for gestational ages 22-50 weeks

neonatal_calculations.clinical_scenario: 28-week gestational age extremely preterm infant, small for gestational age
neonatal_calculations.source: Fenton TR. A new growth chart for preterm infants. Arch Dis Child Fetal Neonatal Ed. 2013;98(5):F394-F398
neonatal_calculations.calculation_method: Weight 0.9 kg at 28 weeks places at 10th percentile (SGA)
neonatal_calculations.notes: SGA status may indicate placental insufficiency or fetal infection; close monitoring needed

neonatal_calculations.clinical_scenario: 35-week late preterm infant with appropriate growth for gestational age
neonatal_calculations.source: Fenton TR. A new growth chart for preterm infants. Arch Dis Child Fetal Neonatal Ed. 2013;98(5):F394-F398
neonatal_calculations.calculation_method: Weight 2.2 kg at 35 weeks is at 50th percentile
neonatal_calculations.notes: Late preterm (35-37 weeks) generally have better outcomes than extremely preterm

neonatal_calculations.clinical_scenario: Term newborn at 40 weeks with normal weight
neonatal_calculations.source: Fenton TR. A new growth chart for preterm infants. Arch Dis Child Fetal Neonatal Ed. 2013;98(5):F394-F398
neonatal_calculations.calculation_method: Weight 3.5 kg at 40 weeks (term) is at 50th percentile
neonatal_calculations.notes: Fenton charts extend to 50 weeks for postnatal assessment of preterm infants

neonatal_calculations.clinical_scenario: Stable preterm 32-week infant, no acute physiologic derangements
neonatal_calculations.source: Richardson DK, et al. SNAP-II: Score for Neonatal Acute Physiology, version 2. Pediatrics. 2001;107(1):33-40
neonatal_calculations.calculation_method: Low mean BP (+2), normal temp (0), mild acidosis (+1), adequate pO2 (+2), no seizures (0), good UOP (+3) = 8 points
neonatal_calculations.notes: Low SNAP-II indicates good prognosis; most infants with score <10 survive

neonatal_calculations.clinical_scenario: Critically ill term infant with sepsis and multi-organ dysfunction
neonatal_calculations.source: Richardson DK, et al. SNAP-II: Score for Neonatal Acute Physiology, version 2. Pediatrics. 2001;107(1):33-40
neonatal_calculations.calculation_method: Severe hypotension (+22), moderate hypothermia (+9), severe acidosis (+13), severe hypoxemia (+16), seizures present (+5), oliguria (+15) = 80 (capped estimate)
neonatal_calculations.notes: High SNAP-II score indicates critical illness; mortality risk >40%; requires ICU support

neonatal_calculations.clinical_scenario: Preterm infant with mild respiratory distress syndrome and transient hypotension
neonatal_calculations.source: Richardson DK, et al. SNAP-II: Score for Neonatal Acute Physiology, version 2. Pediatrics. 2001;107(1):33-40
neonatal_calculations.calculation_method: Low mean BP (+4), normal temp (0), moderate acidosis (+3), low pO2 (+6), no seizures (0), moderate oliguria (+5) = 18 points
neonatal_calculations.notes: Moderate SNAP-II: moderately increased mortality risk but with appropriate treatment often recovers

tolerance_explanation.rationale: 2% tolerance for growth/scoring calculations accounts for:
factors: Use of simplified scoring algorithms vs. complete nomograms
